item.title: Andy Burnham’s Cringe Britannia – “The vibes may have changed, but naff all else has. So ends the most boring coup in British political history. Andy Burnham is our new prime minister, Keir Starmer’s
item.description: Andy Burnham’s Cringe Britannia The vibes may have changed, but naff all else has. i Getty Tom Slater Editor 20th July 2026 i Getty Share Topics Politics UK Want unlimited, ad-free access? Become a spiked supporter. So ends the most boring coup in British political history. Andy Burnham is our new prime minister, Keir Starmer’s leaving van is on its way to north London, and we’re all left wondering whether this latest round of musical chairs will make the blindest bit of difference. Time was, PM
item.content:encoded: Andy Burnham’s Cringe Britannia The vibes may have changed, but naff all else has. i Getty Tom Slater Editor 20th July 2026 i Getty Share Topics Politics UK Want unlimited, ad-free access? Become a spiked supporter. So ends the most boring coup in British political history. Andy Burnham is our new prime minister, Keir Starmer’s leaving van is on its way to north London, and we’re all left wondering whether this latest round of musical chairs will make the blindest bit of difference. Time was, PMs were ousted mid-term over crises, over defining political questions, over something of consequence. This time around we seem to have something more akin to a summer makeover – a Paul Smith glow-up of a Labour Party totally out of ideas. Burnham has certainly reset the vibes. The expensive, donor-purchased suits are out. (The new PM reportedly intends to keep it smart-casual when he’s not addressing parliament.) Starmer’s embattled supply teacher has been replaced by Burnham’s Radio 6 Music dad. But politically speaking, we seem destined for more of the same dysfunction, idiocy and anti-democracy. That Starmer came to power when he did – just two years ago, though it has aged us all immeasurably – is faintly tragicomic looking back on it. A creature of the liberal-left, cosmopolitan, eco-obsessed state reigning over a people smarting from broken borders, sky-high energy bills and a not-so-sneaking suspicion that the elites regard them as scum. A nation looking for inspiration and leadership lumbered with a man who would struggle to gin up a biddable scout group. It was never going to end well. Starmer proved himself psychologically incapable of doing what is necessary to set Britain on a more prosperous, self-confident path. For doing so would mean dispensing with Net Zero, mass immigration, international ‘human rights’ law – the panoply of failed orthodoxies from which our political classes continue to draw their phoney moral superiority. Enjoying spiked? Why not make an instant, one-off donation? We are funded by you. Thank you! £5 £10 £20 £50 Choose an amount Enter your amount £ Donate now Please wait... Thank you! Will Burnham be any different? Will he ’eck. He spoke in his brief, cliché-ridden address outside Downing Street today about finally moving on from Thatcherism, apparently convinced that a few more public-private bus networks would represent a radical new economic model. In truth, he is an unthinking adherent of the real ruling-class ideology of our time – the Third Way worship of technocracy over democracy, multiculturalism over nationhood, and environmental piety over economic growth. The contradiction is glaring. Burnham is simultaneously posing as a steady pair of hands and a decisive break with how politics has been done since the 1980s. When he spoke outside No10, he had one eye on the markets, jittery at any perceived ‘lurch to the left’, and another on his backbench MPs, for whom virtue-signalling and infinity welfare is now the closest thing they have to an ideology. Podcast Leave gamblers alone Last Orders Thus, he spoke one minute of ‘regain[ing] our stability once again’, and the next of a ‘circuit breaker for Britain, bringing forward the biggest changes in the last 40 years’. Those asking how he intends to achieve either were left in suspense. Apparently, he’ll set out his actual policies in the coming days, presumably once he’s had a chance to come up with some. He has pledged to end rough sleeping, though – something he ultimately failed to do as mayor of Greater Manchester. The speech was bland in rhetoric and starved of any detail, and purposefully so. At least we were spared the bopping along to New Order and Smash Hits-style social-media interviews that have accompanied his coronation so far. Welcome to Andy Burnham’s Cringe Britannia, where we know more about his guitar-playing prowess than what he actually intends to do with the country; where Things Will Only Get Worse, only to a more Madchester-infused soundtrack. Constitutionally speaking, the mood music is all he is really allowed to change. Given he has no mandate of his own, and says he won’t call an early election, Burnham is, for now, doomed to tinker around the edges of Starmerism, all while pretending otherwise. So he won’t lift the ban on new oil and gas projects, but he will try his darndest to speed up the existing ones. We’re told he will scrap Starmer’s hated digital-ID scheme, but he is minded to ban VPNs. A new dawn this isn’t. All this reminds us of the man’s total lack of democratic legitimacy. Only the good people of Makerfield have had an opportunity to vote for him, in last month’s by-election, which ultimately sent Starmer packing. Burnham didn’t even stand for parliament on Labour’s 2024 manifesto. He stood unopposed in the Labour leadership race, too, meaning even party members didn’t get to have their say. Now he comes to power with parliament in recess until September, sparing him any democratic scrutiny for his first month-and-a-half in post. At least the media have gotten over their initial bout of giddiness and are starting to ask tougher questions of our unelected PM. Recommended The staggering joylessness of anti-Zionism Brendan O'Neill I bet it won’t even take six weeks for the shine to come off him. Before long, to paraphrase Burnham’s beloved Smiths, the country will be looking back on Our Andy’s coup and wondering, what difference did it make? Tom Slater is editor of spiked. Follow him on X: @Tom_Slater_. Get unlimited access to spiked You’ve hit your monthly free article limit. Support spiked and get unlimited access. Support or Already a supporter? item.title: Who Watches the Watchers? – “Trust is important. Verification is essential.” – Brian C. Joondeph
item.description: Advertisement Advertisement Culture > History Link copied Who Watches the Watchers? Trust is important. Verification is essential. Brian C. Joondeph | July 20, 2026 chatgpt chatgpt Getting your Trinity Audio player ready... More than 2,000 years ago, the Roman poet Juvenal posed a timeless question: Quis custodiet ipsos custodes? — “Who will guard the guards themselves?” or “Who watches the watchers?” Advertisement The Romans could never have imagined artificial intelligence, facial recognition,
item.content:encoded: Advertisement Advertisement Culture > History Link copied Who Watches the Watchers? Trust is important. Verification is essential. Brian C. Joondeph | July 20, 2026 chatgpt chatgpt Getting your Trinity Audio player ready... More than 2,000 years ago, the Roman poet Juvenal posed a timeless question: Quis custodiet ipsos custodes? — “Who will guard the guards themselves?” or “Who watches the watchers?” Advertisement The Romans could never have imagined artificial intelligence, facial recognition, or nationwide networks of automated license-plate readers. Yet their question may be even more relevant today than it was three millennia ago. Advertisement Image created by ChatGPT Recent debate over Flock Safety cameras illustrates why. Advertisement The cameras have become an invaluable law enforcement tool. A stolen vehicle that crosses several jurisdictions can now be identified in minutes rather than days. An abducted child can sometimes be located before the child reaches another state. Investigators can reconstruct a suspect's movements at speeds unimaginable only a generation ago. These advances are remarkable and save lives and solve crimes. Advertisement Few Americans would willingly return to the days when solving a crime depended solely on eyewitness accounts or grainy surveillance footage from convenience stores. Yet reports have surfaced that individual officers allegedly used these systems to monitor former spouses, girlfriends, or acquaintances for purely personal reasons. If true, those actions are not an indictment of the technology or of law enforcement as a whole. They are a reminder of a far older truth: every concentration of power invites the possibility of abuse. Advertisement Technology doesn’t change human nature. It amplifies it. Advertisement Throughout history, every transformative invention has brought both promise and peril. Fire allowed our ancestors to cook food, survive harsh winters, and build civilization. It also destroyed cities. Water sustains every living creature on Earth. Floods and tsunamis destroy entire communities. Even drinking too much water can be fatal. Electricity powers hospitals, schools, factories, and our homes. It can also be deadly. Nuclear physics brought us clean electrical power capable of supplying entire cities. It also produced weapons capable of destroying civilization. Artificial intelligence can help physicians diagnose disease, assist students with learning, help travelers navigate foreign countries, and help writers organize their thoughts. It can also generate convincing misinformation, fabricate legal citations, or automate fraud on an unprecedented scale. The technology itself is rarely the issue. The people using it—and the safeguards governing them — are. Unfortunately, discussions about emerging technologies often become polarized. One side insists the technology should be embraced without hesitation, while the other argues it should be feared or even prohibited. Neither approach reflects reality. Whether we like it or not, modern life is already built on surveillance and digital tracking. Every airline passenger presents identification and is photographed. Smartphones constantly reveal our location. Credit cards, toll roads, and countless online transactions leave permanent electronic footprints. The idea that Americans can somehow “go off the grid” is largely a nostalgic fantasy. That ship sailed years ago. The real question is not whether technology exists. It is who controls it, how it is used, and what safeguards prevent misuse. The risks posed by powerful technologies generally fall into three categories: malicious misuse, human error, and technological flaws. A rogue police officer abusing a surveillance database constitutes malicious misuse. An investigator entering the wrong license plate or drawing the wrong conclusion is human error. Even when everyone acts in good faith, facial recognition software, artificial intelligence, and other complex systems remain imperfect. Perhaps an even greater danger is assuming that sophisticated technology is somehow infallible. It isn’t. Artificial intelligence occasionally invents facts. Attorneys have embarrassed themselves by submitting legal briefs that cite entirely fictitious court cases generated by AI. The technology was impressive, but it simply wasn’t correct. Facial recognition has improved enormously over the past decade, but no algorithm is perfect. A poor-quality image, unusual lighting, or an unfortunate similarity between two individuals could generate a false investigative lead. Modern medicine offers a useful analogy. No competent physician makes a diagnosis based solely on a single laboratory value or imaging study. We correlate laboratory results with the patient’s history, physical examination, and additional testing. A screening test points us toward a diagnosis; it does not establish one beyond question. Technology should inform human judgment, not replace it. The same principle should apply to surveillance technologies. A facial recognition match or license plate detection should trigger an investigation, not conclude one. Nor should every concern be treated as an attack on law enforcement. Medicine has its rogue physicians. Law has unethical attorneys. Finance has dishonest brokers. Politics has corrupt officeholders. No profession is immune to bad actors. We do not conclude that all physicians are incompetent because one doctor commits malpractice. Nor should isolated misconduct by individual officers serve as an indictment of the thousands of honorable men and women who wear a badge every day and use these technologies responsibly to protect their communities. The issue is accountability. In medicine, every access to an electronic medical record is logged. Hospitals routinely audit those records. Years ago, hospital employees made headlines for improperly accessing the medical records of celebrities out of curiosity. Those audit trails made the misconduct discoverable, and consequences followed. If surveillance systems maintain similar audit logs — as Flock reportedly does — the obvious question is: Who is reviewing them? An audit trail is valuable only if someone conducts the audit. Imagine a system in which every database search is permanently recorded, random audits are routine, unauthorized access results in suspension or termination, and deliberate misuse carries criminal penalties. Public confidence would increase dramatically without diminishing the technology’s legitimate usefulness. The technology wouldn’t change. Only accountability would. The Framers of the Constitution understood this principle long before computers existed. They didn’t design a government on the assumption that those in power would always act honorably. They designed it around checks and balances because they understood human nature. James Madison famously observed that if men were angels, no government would be needed. Neither are algorithms. Nor are those entrusted with them. Perhaps the greatest mistake we can make is demanding perfection. No technology created by human beings will ever be flawless. Airplanes occasionally crash despite extraordinary safety records. Nuclear reactors fail despite layers of engineering safeguards. Self-driving vehicles make mistakes. Human beings certainly do. The answer is not to abandon progress. The answer is to build systems that anticipate imperfection. Good engineering assumes that components will fail. Good medicine assumes that diagnoses can be wrong. Good government should assume that power, if left unchecked, will eventually be abused. Good aviation assumes that pilots can make mistakes. The challenge before us is therefore not whether to embrace new technologies. That decision has already been made. Flock cameras, artificial intelligence, facial recognition, drones, and innovations we have not yet imagined will become increasingly woven into everyday life. The enduring question is the one Juvenal posed twenty centuries ago. Who watches the watchers? The answer cannot be simply “trust them.” It must be Ronald Reagan’s famous advice, “Trust but verify.” Powerful technologies deserve equally robust oversight. That oversight protects not only individual liberty but also the integrity of the institutions that use these remarkable tools. When the public knows there are meaningful safeguards against abuse, confidence grows rather than eroding. Progress without accountability breeds distrust. Accountability without progress breeds stagnation. Liberty requires both innovation and oversight. Brian C. Joondeph, MD, is a Colorado-based ophthalmologist who writes frequently about medicine, science, and public policy. Follow Brian: Twitter @retinaldoctor Substack Dr. Brian’s Substack Truth Social @BrianJoondeph LinkedIn @Brian Joondeph Email [email protected] Related Topics: History View & Add Comments SUPPORT AMERICAN THINKER Now more than ever, the ability to speak our minds is crucial to the republic we cherish. If what you see on American Thinker resonates with you, please consider supporting our work with a donation of as much or as little as you can give. Every dollar contributed helps us pay our staff and keep our ideas heard and our voices strong. Thank you. item.title: Oh Dear, Condo Prices Fell by 15% to 33% in 30 Bigger Cities Already. Some Dropped Back to 2006 Levels – “In another 39 bigger cities, condo prices fell by 8% to 14%. A massive hangover after a histor
item.description: In another 39 bigger cities, condo prices fell by 8% to 14%. A massive hangover after a historic Condo Bubble. By Wolf Richter for WOLF STREET. Condos have, let’s say, some issues after the breath-taking price explosions from mid-2020 to mid-2022 of 50%, 60%, or even 70% in some cities. item.content:encoded: In another 39 bigger cities, condo prices fell by 8% to 14%. A massive hangover after a historic Condo Bubble. By Wolf Richter for WOLF STREET. Condos have, let’s say, some issues after the breath-taking price explosions from mid-2020 to mid-2022 of 50%, 60%, or even 70% in some cities. In the 10 years to the peak, prices had soared by 180% to 350% in these markets. And these bubbles have started to deflate. And the price drops have been spreading relentlessly and have been getting steeper. In 30 bigger markets, prices of mid-tier condos through June have already dropped by 15% to 33% from their respective peaks. In 9 of them, prices have dropped by 21% to 33%. Most of their peaks occurred in the years between 2022 and 2024. These are starting to be substantial declines over a multiyear period. These 30 markets are depicted in the charts below. In another 39 bigger cities prices, have dropped by 8% to 14%. They didn’t make the 15% cutoff to have their own chart. But they have their own table (see below the charts). In several of these markets, condo prices have now dropped below their highs in 2006, below the peaks of Housing Bubble 1, and are back where they’d been 20 years ago. In a few other markets, prices have dropped close to their 2006 peaks. Those charts are marked with a red line. There are also many smaller markets where condo prices have dropped just as much or more, but that are not included here because the markets are too small. Most of the markets here are “cities.” But the list of 15% to 33% decliners also includes four counties where the cities – though household names – are too small to be included individually. And it includes one metropolitan statistical area, the Lakeland-Winter Haven MSA in Florida, for the same reason. In some densely populated big cities, condos and co-ops make up a big part or the majority of home sales. In most other markets, condos are a relatively small portion of home sales. Methodology and data: These prices here are seasonally adjusted three-month averages of “mid-tier” condos and co-ops from the Zillow Home Value Index (ZHVI), which is backward-looking index, based on millions of data points in Zillow’s “Database of All Homes,” including from public records (tax data), MLS, brokerages, local Realtor Associations, real-estate agents, and households across the US. It includes pricing data for off-market deals and for-sale-by-owner deals. The Condo Bust by market in 30 charts. The tables for each market below show from left to right: price decline from the peak, change from prior month (MoM), change year-over-year (YoY), and remaining increase since January 2000. Cape Coral-Fort Myers, FL, MSA, Condo Prices From July 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -33% -0.4% -12.1% 128% Back to November 2005, that was 21 years ago. Two enormous speculative bubbles, two busts. Oakland, CA, City, Condo Home Prices From May 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -32% -0.4% -10.3% 138% Prices are back to where they’d fist been 21 years ago, in mid-2005. Prices are down a lot, but are still very high after the huge surge in the years before mid-2022. St. Petersburg, Fl, City, Condo Prices From Oct 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -29% -0.7% -10.7% 177% Austin, TX, City, Condo Prices From Jul 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -28% -0.8% -6.0% 103% Fort Myers, FL, City, Condo Prices From July 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -27% -0.4% -11% 119% Back to 2006. Sarasota County, FL, Condo & Co-ops Prices From Jun 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -24% -0.2% -7.6% 129% Back to 2006. Tampa, FL, City, Condo Prices From Sep 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -21% -0.6% -9.1% 245% Garland, TX, City, Condo Prices From July 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -21% -0.8% -12.4% 204% Jacksonville, FL, City, Condo Prices From Nov 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -21% -0.7% -7.9% 140% Detroit, MI, City, Condo Prices From Sep 2021 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -19% 0.2% -7.0% 244% Denver, CO, City, Condo Prices From Jul 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -19% -0.6% -6.3% 127% Aurora, CO, City, Condo Prices From Jul 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -18% -0.9% -7.2% 190% Arlington, TX, City, Condo Prices From Jun 2024 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -18% -0.6% -5.4% 225% Collier County (Naples), FL, Condo & Co-ops Prices From Jun 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -18% -0.5% -6.1% 152% Orlando, FL, City, Condo Prices From Jan 2024 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -17% -0.7% -8.3% 146.7% Prices are back where they’d been at the peak of Housing Bubble 1 in the fall of 2006. Manhattan, NY, Condo & Co-Op Prices From Jun 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -17% -0.4% 2.2% 216% Seattle, WA, City, Condo Prices From Jun 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -17% -0.9% -4.9% 128% Lakeland-Winter Haven, FL, MSA, Condo Prices From July 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -17% -0.5% -7.9% 122% Raleigh, NC, City, Condo Prices From July 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -17% -0.5% -8.3% 131.4% Hayward, CA, City, Condo Prices From July 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -17% -0.7% -8.4% 174% Hayward, Oakland (see above, -32%), and Fremont (see below, -15%) are among a bunch of cities that make up the East Bay. San Mateo County, CA, Condo & Co-op Prices From Jun 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -16% -0.3% -4.3% 191% San Mateo County makes up northern Silicon Valley. Plano, TX, City, Condo Prices From Aug 2023 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -16% -1.0% -8.6% 122% Port Saint Lucie, FL, City, Condo Prices From July 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -16% -0.4% -7.0% 226.9% Mesa, AZ, City, Condo Prices From Aug 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -16% -0.7% -4.7% 196% Fremont, CA, City, Condo Prices From May 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -15% -1.0% -6.8% 195.1% Portland, OR, City, Condo Prices From Jun 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -15% -0.7% -4.0% 104% Phoenix, AZ, City, Condo Prices From Aug 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -15% -0.5% -4.0% 227% Reno, NV, City, Condo Prices From Jun 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -15% 0.0% -3.5% 241% Chandler, AZ, City, Condo Prices From Aug 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2000 -15% -0.7% -4.3% 203.2% Boise, ID, City, Condo Prices From Jun 2022 peak MoM YoY Since 2001 -15% -0.4% -1% 221% Where condo prices fell less than 15%. In many cities, condo prices have dropped by less than 15% or less, and they didn’t make the 15% cutoff for the table above and for the charts here. Here is a sample list of 39 bigger cities where prices have dropped by 8% to 14% from their respective peaks. Several of them are only one or two bad months away from joining the above list, including one of the biggest cities in the country, Houston. Dallas isn’t far behind. Mid-tier condo prices fell by 8% to 14% Market Since peak Year of peak 1 Houston, TX -14% 2024 2 Queens, NY -14% 2022 3 San Antonio, TX -14% 2024 4 Tempe, AZ -14% 2022 5 Glendale, AZ -14% 2022 6 Huntsville, AL -14% 2022 7 Dallas, TX -14% 2023 8 Scottsdale, AZ -14% 2022 9 Irving, TX -13% 2023 10 Sacramento, CA -13% 2022 11 Colorado Springs, CO -13% 2022 12 Stockton, CA -13% 2022 13 Corpus Christi, TX -13% 2023 14 Fort Lauderdale, FL -12% 2022 15 Modesto, CA -12% 2022 16 Henderson, NV -12% 2022 17 Las Vegas, NV -12% 2022 18 Spokane, WA -11% 2022 19 Nashville, TN -10% 2022 20 Atlanta, GA -10% 2023 21 Washington, DC -10% 2022 22 Elk Grove, CA -10% 2022 23 New Orleans -10% 2022 24 Salt Lake City, UT -9% 2022 25 San Francisco, CA -9% 2022 26 Memphis, TN -9% 2024 27 Fort Worth, TX -9% 2024 28 San Diego, CA -9% 2023 29 Los Angeles, CA -8% 2022 30 Minneapolis, MN -8% 2021 31 San Jose, CA -8% 2022 32 Miami, FL -8% 2023 33 Marietta GA -8% 2024 34 Lubbock, TX -8% 2022 35 Oklahoma City, OK -8% 2023 36 Tucson, AZ -8% 2023 37 Wilmington, NC -8% 2022 38 St. Louis, MO -8% 2023 39 Long Beach, CA -8% 2023 A reminder of the special issues condos face. Condos play several roles, depending on the owner: Some people buy condos as a home to live in an urban center or along the shore, to enjoy the big views, nice amenities, or central location. They value the worry-free living, such as not having to mess with maintenance, repairs, and yardwork; or having staff at a desk by the front door. Some value not having to climb stairs, etc. Others buy condos as rental properties as a way to get into the multifamily rental business, or they try their hand at short-term vacation rentals. Or they buy them as vacation homes. Others, especially nonresident foreign investors, buy condos to park some cash in the US and watch the price spiral higher from a distance. It’s these investors and speculators that make condos particularly speculative and prices subject to huge bubbles and busts. Some of the special issues: Over the long term, land appreciates, most buildings depreciate to zero and are eventually torn down. The land that big condo buildings sit on can be very valuable, but each condo owner only owns a tiny slice of it. The rest of their investment is in the building. A single-family house may sit on less valuable land, but the homeowner gets 100% of any appreciation of the land. Prices that exploded over the past few years ended up being way too high, once the mania settled down. Hefty special assessments – or the fear of them – for long-neglected major repairs dog some older condo buildings. Big increases in HOA fees at many properties, partly driven by spiking insurance costs in natural disaster zones, add substantially to the monthly costs of condos. If a condo building is on Fannie Mae’s Blacklist, financing a unit in that building gets very difficult, and sales may be limited to cash buyers who’ll exact their pound of flesh. The Free Money has ended, and mortgage rates are roughly back to a normal range. Buyers of single-family homes face the same issue. Foreign-based owners who’ve had it with the US and want to sell. And there are fewer foreign-based buyers. Investors in condos as rental properties are facing stiff competition from a wave of newly completed higher-end apartment buildings that developers are trying to find tenants for. And in case you missed it: Pending Home Sales Plunge to Near-Record Low in the Data, Hit Low in the West. Since then, Mortgage Rates Rose Further Enjoy reading WOLF STREET and want to support it? You can donate. I appreciate it immensely. Click on the mug to find out how: To subscribe to WOLF STREET... Enter your email address to receive notifications of new articles by email. It's free. item.title: Trump’s 18-Month Deadline To Cut Electricity Prices in Half Has Arrived. Rates Are Up 18%. – ‘Yet another broken campaign promise.” – Jeff Luse
item.description: Donald Trump pledged on the campaign trail that he would slash electricity prices in half within 18 months of returning to the White House. That self-imposed deadline is today, and—as with many of the president's promises—he appears to have fallen short of his goal. From Trump's inauguration in January 2025 to April 2026, the most recent month for which government data are available, residential electricity rates have risen by an eye-watering 18 percent. From April 2025 to April 2026, rates jump
item.content:encoded: Donald Trump pledged on the campaign trail that he would slash electricity prices in half within 18 months of returning to the White House. That self-imposed deadline is today, and—as with many of the president's promises—he appears to have fallen short of his goal. From Trump's inauguration in January 2025 to April 2026, the most recent month for which government data are available, residential electricity rates have risen by an eye-watering 18 percent. From April 2025 to April 2026, rates jumped by 7.3 percent, or about twice the rate of inflation, according to the Energy Information Administration. Unfortunately, for many consumers, there is not much relief in sight. Last week, PJM Interconnection, the nation's largest grid operator, announced the results of its annual capacity auction, which determined the cost to procure power in the region for the 2028–2029 delivery year. The cost of the latest auction hit $16.4 billion, tying the previous auction's record-high price and all but locking customers into historically high rates. Nationally, utilities in the second quarter of 2026 "asked state regulators to approve $9.2 billion in rate hikes, up 26% from the $7.3 billion in rate increase proposals filed in the same period last year," reports Utility Dive. While it may be easy to place most of the blame on Trump, given the brashness of his promise, the president inherited a slow-moving train crash of unprecedented load growth from the data center boom, which coincided with the closure of coal and natural gas power plants. Together, this has led to rate hikes and warnings about grid reliability. "It was an impossible promise to deliver on because there really isn't that much federal government involvement in retail rates," says Travis Fisher, director of energy and environmental policy studies at the Cato Institute. "It was always going to be a steep uphill climb." Indeed, while the federal government—primarily through the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission—sets the rules governing interstate transmission lines and wholesale markets, what consumers pay each month for electricity is determined by a myriad of other factors, including fuel prices and the cost of building local distribution infrastructure, such as poles and wires. And though the price of wholesale electricity depends on where you live, states retain significant authority over many of the costs that make it to utility bills. Trump does bear at least some of the responsibility, though. His trade war, for instance, has made critical components more expensive. Like many other goods, most transformers in the United States are imported, primarily from Mexico and China. (At one point in the past 18 months, goods from the latter faced a 147 percent import tax.) Electrical steel, meanwhile, comes in from South Korea, India, and Japan, all of which were subject to "reciprocal" tariffs until the Supreme Court struck them down earlier this year. When these duties were still in place, the investment firm Morningstar warned that they "directly threaten the ambitious agenda to upgrade and expand the U.S. power grid." With them struck down, consumers are still paying for these duties, as well as the 15 percent tariff rate the administration maintains on "some electrical grid equipment," according to Utility Dive. As the administration has increased input costs, it has also made it harder for certain energy sources to connect to the grid. Beginning on Day 1, Trump blocked leasing for offshore wind in federal waters, which a federal judge later struck down. In March, the administration unveiled a new strategy to stop offshore wind: paying developers to abandon these projects. That month, the federal government agreed to give TotalEnergies nearly $1 billion—a reimbursement for what it paid for its leases—to terminate planned offshore wind plants in New York and North Carolina and instead invest that money in fossil-fuel projects. The federal government has since reached similar deals to terminate four other offshore wind projects, reports The New York Times. Fisher, who is "not a fan of offshore wind" because "it pretty much only gets built with subsidies and mandates," is also not a fan of these agreements. He says these plants could have added "substantial downward pressure" and reduced PJM's auction price. Notably, even when the administration has done the opposite and forced power plants to stay open, consumers have been left to foot the bill. Meanwhile, data centers' electricity use added $6 billion to PJM's auction. With more Americans becoming increasingly anti–data center, states have implemented stringent regulations on these facilities. Last week, New York Democratic Gov. Kathy Hochul issued the nation's first moratorium on data centers. New York's ban follows Maine's efforts to implement a similar measure that passed the Legislature but was vetoed by Democratic Gov. Janet Mills in April. There are better solutions than a moratorium. In March, Trump unveiled a Ratepayer Protection Pledge that would require signatories to front the cost of grid upgrades, water infrastructure, and power generation for data centers. The pledge has not really made an impact, but that could change as more governors and utilities get on board. Some experts, including Fisher, advocate a Consumer Regulated Electricity (CRE) model that allows large-load customers (such as data centers) to connect directly to unregulated power plants independent of the grid. Fisher says this approach is more "nimble and innovative." He also calls it a "hedge against the political risk" associated with data centers. New Hampshire adopted CRE-adjacent rules last year. And in January, Sen. Tom Cotton (R–Ark.) introduced a bill that would make it easier for data centers to plug directly into a power plant. Solutions like that could provide a fix to the affordability crisis. But even they are unlikely to provide the kind of relief that Trump promised and failed to deliver.

item.title: Islam and America Are Incompatible – “Here is a declaration of Islamic supremacy Americans can no longer ignore.” – J.B. Shurk
item.description: Advertisement Advertisement Religion > Islam Link copied Islam and America Are Incompatible Here is a declaration of Islamic supremacy Americans can no longer ignore. J.B. Shurk | July 20, 2026 Robert Couse-Baker Robert Couse-Baker Getting your Trinity Audio player ready... Patriotic Americans have been celebrating their country’s two-hundred-fiftieth birthday all year. Although an increasing number of Democrats now despise America and even admit to feeling revulsion at the sight of Old Glory wa
item.content:encoded: Advertisement Advertisement Religion > Islam Link copied Islam and America Are Incompatible Here is a declaration of Islamic supremacy Americans can no longer ignore. J.B. Shurk | July 20, 2026 Robert Couse-Baker Robert Couse-Baker Getting your Trinity Audio player ready... Patriotic Americans have been celebrating their country’s two-hundred-fiftieth birthday all year. Although an increasing number of Democrats now despise America and even admit to feeling revulsion at the sight of Old Glory waving in the wind, 2026 has been an incredible opportunity to commemorate in grand fashion America’s founding principles, accomplishments, and enduring legacy. Advertisement Perhaps it is the heightened sense of American patriotism hanging in the air this year that is leading many ordinary citizens to become much more angry about the anti-American forces working to “fundamentally transform” the nation, to borrow President Barack Obama’s menacing description of the kind of “change” he seeks. On social media platforms last week, videos spread of a Home Depot shopping experience in Dearborn, Michigan. Signs throughout the store were in Arabic. The original poster claimed (and later reporting confirmed) that major retailers throughout the Midwestern city — including Walmart, Costco, Kroger, and Albertsons — also include signs written in Arabic. The poster concluded, “This is not assimilation, this is an Islamic Takeover of America.” Advertisement Commenters who responded to the videos were furious. One warned, “What are we doing, America? Are we capitulating to these Muslim invaders? Watering down our American standards by plastering signs in Arabic? ENGLISH is the official language of America.” In describing the social media firestorm, journalist Bob Unruh pointed out that Dearborn, Michigan, now has a majority Muslim population, a Muslim police force, Muslim city officials, and official Muslim holidays. Dearborn’s Muslim mayor recently “told a Christian he’s not welcome in the city.” Dearborn’s Muslim police chief recently “bragged about how a large increase in the number of Arab police officers was better serving the city and ended his boast with a Muslim prayer.” Dearborn recently renamed a city street in honor of a resident who has allegedly supported Islamic terrorism in the past. Advertisement Dearborn, Michigan, the hometown of Henry Ford and his Ford Motor Company, has been conquered. There is no other way to describe the reality of the situation. No matter how much Democrats pretend that Islam is compatible with American ideals, it is not. Christians founded America. Christian virtue provided the cement for America’s foundations in personal liberty. And the growth of Islamism in the United States represents a threat to the nation’s survival. In one particularly egregious example of Barack Obama’s anti-American propaganda, the former president used to tell listeners a demonstrable lie — that Muslims played an instrumental role in America’s founding. In 2010, he said, “Islam has always been part of America.” In 2014, Obama thanked Muslims for “building the very fabric of our nation” and insisted that they represented “the core of our democracy.” Obama even claimed that Americans share “common values” with Islam. Advertisement As many pundits pointed out at the time, the Founding Fathers were quite clear in their writings that they considered Islam a dangerous creed at odds with Americans’ love for God and liberty. When President Thomas Jefferson sent the Navy to the Barbary Coast to protect American merchant ships from Islamic pirates, he took time to read the Quran and correctly concluded that Muslims are commanded to “plunder and enslave” non-Muslims. When Tripoli renewed attacks on American ships a decade later, President John Adams wrote that Islam’s founder and prophet was “a military fanatic,” while describing Islamic law as “contemptible.” President John Quincy Adams later wrote that Islam is “violence and lust: to exalt the brutal over the spiritual part of human nature.” America’s sixth president argued, “The precept of the Koran is perpetual war against all who deny that Muhammad is the prophet of God,” rendering Islam inherently incompatible with America’s Judeo-Christian ethic, civic virtue, and democratic peace. A hundred years later, Winston Churchill wrote of the manifest difference between Christianity and Islam: Advertisement “Christianity, however degraded and distorted by cruelty and intolerance, must always exert a modifying influence on men’s passions, and protect them from the more violent forms of fanatical fever, as we are protected from smallpox by vaccination. But the Mahommedan religion increases, instead of lessening, the fury of intolerance. It was originally propagated by the sword, and ever since its votaries have been subject, above the people of all other creeds, to this form of madness. In a moment the fruits of patient toil, the prospects of material prosperity, the fear of death itself, are flung aside…All rational considerations are forgotten…they become…as dangerous and sensible as mad dogs: fit only to be treated as such…convulsed in an ecstasy of religious bloodthirstiness…civilisation is confronted with militant Mahommedanism. The forces of progress clash with those of reaction. The religion of blood and war is face to face with that of peace. Luckily the religion of peace is usually the better armed.” Despite a half-century of anti-Western propaganda that has aggressively tried to rebrand Islam as “the religion of peace,” it is important to note that Churchill correctly described Christianity as the “religion of peace” and Islam as the “religion of blood and war.” While Barack Obama and his fellow Democrats spend inordinate resources attempting to rewrite history, there has never been anything “peaceful” about Islam. It is also antithetical to America’s founding principles. Advertisement Driving this last point home, Islamic supremacist Linda Sarsour just released a video plea for Americans to replace the Declaration of Independence. As real Americans know, the Declaration is distinct from the U.S. Constitution. Although it expresses Americans’ founding principles, it is not a governing charter or legal document that can be repealed. Sarsour doesn’t care. Calling the two-hundred-fiftieth anniversary of the Declaration of Independence “quote unquote ‘America’s birthday,’” she argues that “marginalized” people should “want to build a document that is our values and our principles.” She then tells her followers that they will “fight back” and “win.” From Sarsour’s point of view, then, “winning” is about aborting America’s birthday and replacing the United States with some foreign nation founded on her Islamic supremacist values. The hijab-wearing Linda Sarsour is a member of the Democratic Socialists of America, an organization that embraces revolutionary violence. She helped elect New York City’s Muslim Mayor Zohran Mamdani. She is working to elect Michigan’s Muslim senate candidate, Abdul El-Sayed. She hates America. But because she is one of seven children whom her “Palestinian” immigrant parents had in Brooklyn, the U.S. Supreme Court says that she’s an American citizen. Despite everything Americans have given her, she feels entitled to destroy America on behalf of her Islamic brothers and sisters living in the Middle East. In return for waging war against the United States, Sarsour’s “non-profit” organizations have received millions of dollars from American taxpayers. This is insane. American taxpayers subsidize Islamic supremacists working to conquer the United States. As one angry commenter told Sarsour, “You were fortunate that just because you were born here that you can claim US citizenship. Not a single strand of your DNA is American.” Another called Sarsour’s anti-Declaration of Independence video “subversion” and concluded, “These people have to go.” One thing is certain: The threat of Islam to America is not going away. Decades of open borders policies have invited millions of Muslims into the United States. Some assimilate. Many do not. And when Muslim immigrants become a significant percentage of any local American population, they immediately project their numerical strength. Muslims choose to build mosques next to the World Trade Center site in Manhattan, where Islamic terrorists murdered 3,000 Americans on September 11, 2001. They build giant mosques that overwhelm rural American towns. Muslims blast Islamic calls to prayer over loudspeakers in cities and towns across America. Muslims pull out prayer mats and transform busy public squares into open-air mosques. This is nothing less than a declaration of Islamic supremacy: We are taking over America. Islam and America are incompatible. Image: Robert Couse-Baker via Flickr, CC BY 2.0. Related Topics: Islam View & Add Comments SUPPORT AMERICAN THINKER Now more than ever, the ability to speak our minds is crucial to the republic we cherish. If what you see on American Thinker resonates with you, please consider supporting our work with a donation of as much or as little as you can give. Every dollar contributed helps us pay our staff and keep our ideas heard and our voices strong. Thank you.
